howItWorks.intro: Each state sets its own statute of limitations — the maximum time after an event within which legal proceedings may be initiated.
rules: Written contracts: typically 4–10 years depending on the state
Oral contracts: generally shorter than written, often 2–6 years
Personal injury: usually 2–3 years from the date of injury
Property damage: typically 2–6 years from the date of damage
Discovery rule: in some cases, the clock starts when the injury is discovered, not when it occurred
Tolling: the deadline may be paused if the defendant leaves the state or if the plaintiff is a minor
howItWorks.outro: Missing the statute of limitations almost always means your case will be dismissed. If you believe you have a claim, consult an attorney well before the deadline to preserve your rights.

faq.question: What happens if I miss the statute of limitations?
faq.answer: If you file a lawsuit after the statute of limitations has expired, the defendant can file a motion to dismiss and the court will almost certainly grant it. There are very few exceptions, such as the discovery rule for hidden injuries or tolling for minors.

faq.question: When does the statute of limitations clock start?
faq.answer: Generally, the clock starts on the date of the incident — when the breach of contract occurred, when you were injured, or when property was damaged. Under the 'discovery rule,' it may start when you knew or should have known about the harm.

faq.question: Can the statute of limitations be paused or extended?
faq.answer: Yes. Common reasons for tolling (pausing) include: the plaintiff is a minor, the defendant left the state, the plaintiff was mentally incapacitated, or the defendant concealed the wrongdoing. Each state has specific tolling rules.

faq.question: Is the statute of limitations different for criminal vs civil cases?
faq.answer: Yes. This calculator covers civil case deadlines. Criminal statutes of limitations are set by different laws and are typically longer. Serious crimes like murder often have no statute of limitations at all.

faq.question: Do federal cases have different time limits?
faq.answer: Federal claims have their own statutes of limitations set by federal law. For example, federal employment discrimination claims must be filed with the EEOC within 180 or 300 days. This calculator covers state civil claims only.

faq.question: Does sending a demand letter stop the clock?
faq.answer: No. Sending a demand letter does not pause or restart the statute of limitations. Only filing a lawsuit in court stops the clock. A demand letter is a good step, but it does not substitute for timely filing.
